sanskrit_devanagari:
धूमो रात्रिस्तथा कृष्णः षण्मासा दक्षिणायनम् ।
तत्र चान्द्रमसं ज्योतिर्योगी प्राप्य निवर्तते ॥ २५ ॥
sanskrit_iast:
dhūmo rātris tathā kṛṣṇaḥ ṣaṇmāsā dakṣiṇāyanam
tatra cāndramasaṃ jyotir yogī prāpya nivartate || 25 ||

sanskrit_sources.agreement: exact (canonical body text; vedabase concatenates the two halves; danda restored from gitasupersite)
exact (text body identical; verse-number marker rendered as ।।8.25।।)
exact IAST (verbatim from page; sandhi conventions match GRETIL)

translations.verbatim_quote: The mystic who passes away from this world during the smoke, the night, the fortnight of the waning moon, or the six months when the sun passes to the south reaches the moon planet but again comes back.

The practitioners of Vedic rituals, who pass away during the six months of the sun's southern course, the dark fortnight of the moon, the time of smoke, the night, attain the celestial abodes. After enjoying celestial pleasures, they again return to the earth.

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: In the Third Canto of Śrīmad-Bhāgavatam Kapila Muni mentions that those who are expert in fruitive activities and sacrificial methods on earth attain to the moon at death. These elevated souls live on the moon for about 10,000 years (by demigod calculations) and enjoy life by drinking soma…
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Prabhupada glosses BG 8.25 in the Gaudiya Vaishnava key.

commentaries.translator: Swami Gambhirananda (Advaita Ashrama edition of Sankara-bhasya)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 8.25 Dhuman, smoke; and ratrih night, are the deities presiding over smoke and night. Similarly, krsnah, the dark fornight, means the deity of the dark fortnight. Just as before, by sanmasah daksinayanam the six months of the Southern solstice, also is verily meant a deity. Tatra, followin…
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Shankara reads BG 8.25 in the Advaita non-dual key.

commentaries.translator: Swami Adidevananda (Sri Ramakrishna Math edition of Ramanuja's Gita-bhasya)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 8.25 This denotes the world of the manes etc., described by the term ‘starting with smoke.’ Here the term Yogin connotes one associated with good actions.
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Ramanuja reads BG 8.25 in the Vishishtadvaita devotional-qualified key.

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: Now Lord Krishna explains the path that leads to a return of rebirth in the material existence. When one accedes to death at the times presided by the demigods identified with smoke, with night, with the waning moon and with the suns waning southern course guided on these paths those who p…
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Sridhara provides a synthetic gloss bridging Advaita and devotional readings.

positions.rendering: smoke, night, the dark fortnight, the six months of the sun's southern course
the dark fortnight of the moon, the time of smoke, the night, the six months of the sun's southern course
deities presiding over smoke, night, dark-fortnight, dakṣiṇāyana — the Southern Path; the karmin reaches the lunar realm and returns when merit is exhausted
the world of the manes; the path beginning with smoke; 'yogi' here connotes one associated with good actions, not the jñānin

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: Paired with 8.24 as the dark path (pitṛyāna). Shankara explicitly reads each item as a deity. Ramanuja reads the verse as describing the world of the manes for ritualists, distinct from the jñānin's path. Both ground the reading in Cha. U. 5.10. The verse cannot be operationalised; the engineering analog is silence with the source pack open.

literal_meaning: Smoke, night, the dark fortnight, the six months of the southern course — the yogi who departs in those reaches lunar light and returns.
traditional_meaning_consensus: The dark path (pitṛyāna): smoke, night, the dark fortnight, the six months of dakṣiṇāyana. The yogi (read here as the karmin, the man-of-actions) who departs in these reaches the lunar realm, exhausts the merit, and returns to embodied existence. Shankara: deities of the Southern Path. Ramanuja: this is the world of the manes; 'yogi' here connotes the ritualist, distinct from the jñānin of 8.24.

remaining_gaps: Madhva did not comment on BG 8.25 (per Sarvabhashya tradition).
Ramanuja verbatim — page renders commentary in JS; bhagavad-gita.us static-HTML excerpt captured but full Adidevananda edition would deepen the citation.
